title: Stolen Card Cross-Product Category Exploitation via ProductCD Diversification
narrative: Fraudster uses compromised card credentials to make purchases across multiple product categories (ProductCD types) that the legitimate cardholder never accessed historically. The card is being tested or exploited across W (wallet transactions), C (cash-out), H (home/services), S (subscription), and R (retail) product codes in a short rolling window. Individual transaction amounts and timing appear normal, but C14 (product-category counter) spikes dramatically above the cardholder's historical baseline. The fraudster leverages stolen CVV and billing address to pass merchant verification, but the sudden diversification into unfamiliar product categories reveals unauthorized use. This pattern exploits merchants in different verticals where the cardholder has no purchase history, creating a distinct cross-category signature invisible to single-transaction analysis.
indicators: C14 > 7 (p90), indicating card accessed more distinct ProductCD types than 90% of transactions
card1_distinct_products >= 4 within rolling window, card spans multiple product categories
C1 > 60 (p90), elevated total card-counter showing high aggregated activity
D1 = 0 or D1 < 5, indicating new or recent device-card pairing for this ProductCD type
C11 > 27 (p90), card-email aggregation counter elevated despite clean per-transaction behavior
addr1_ProductCD_count = 1 or 2, billing address unfamiliar with this product category combination
indicator_explanations: The card is being used across more product categories than the vast majority of normal transactions
Card appears in purchases spanning retail, subscriptions, services, and cash-out categories simultaneously
Overall card activity counter is far above typical cardholder baseline across all merchants
This device has little or no history transacting in this product category with this card
Card-email combination shows elevated aggregation despite individual transaction appearing routine
The billing address has minimal or no history purchasing from this product category
summary: Compromised credentials are exploited across diverse ProductCD categories (W, C, H, S, R) the cardholder never accessed historically, creating elevated C14 counters while individual transactions appear normal.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders establish consistent product-category patterns over time. Sudden diversification across multiple unfamiliar ProductCD types signals credential theft where fraudsters test the card across different merchant verticals to maximize extraction before detection.
